content:
# Matthew Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_018_page_001.jpg, MAT_018_page_002.jpg

1. In that hour the disciples came to the Yeshua, saying, "Who then is greatest in the kingdom of the heavens?"

2. And having summoned a child, he set it in the midst of them

3. and said, "Truly I say to you, unless you turn and become as the children, you will not enter into the kingdom of the heavens.

4. Whoever therefore humbles himself as this child, this [one] is the greatest in the kingdom of the heavens.

5. And whoever receives one such child in the name of me receives me.

6. But whoever scandalizes one of these little [ones], the [ones] believing in me, it is better for him that a millstone of a donkey be hung around the neck of him and he be drowned in the depth of the sea.

7. Woe to the world because of the scandals! For it is necessary [for] the scandals to come, but woe to the man through whom the scandal comes.

8. And if the hand of you or the foot of you scandalizes you, cut it off and cast [it] from you; it is better for you to enter into the life crippled or lame than having two hands or two feet to be cast into the eternal fire.

9. And if the eye of you scandalizes you, pluck it out and cast [it] from you; it is better for you one-eyed to enter into the life than having two eyes to be cast into the Gehenna of the fire.

10. See that you do not despise one of these little [ones]; for I say to you that the angels of them in [the] heavens continually see the face of the Father of me, the [one] in [the] heavens.

11. [This verse is absent in Sinaiticus.]

12. What do you think? If there be to a certain man a hundred sheep and one of them wanders, will he not leave the ninety-nine upon the mountains and having gone seek the wandering [one]?

13. And if it happens to find it, truly I say to you that he rejoices over it more than over the ninety-nine not having wandered.

14. So it is not [the] will before the Father of you, the [one] in [the] heavens, that one of these little [ones] perish.

15. And if the brother of you sins against you, go, convict him between you and him alone. If he hears you, you gained the brother of you.

16. But if he does not hear, take along with you still one or two, in order that upon [the] mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established.

17. But if he refuses to hear them, tell [it] to the assembly; and if he also refuses to hear the assembly, let him be to you as the gentile and the tax-collector.

18. Truly I say to you, whatever you bind upon the earth will be having been bound in heaven, and whatever you loose upon the earth will be having been loosed in heaven.

19. Again I say to you that if two of you agree upon the earth concerning any matter which they ask, it will be done for them by the Father of me, the [one] in [the] heavens.

20. For where two or three are gathered into the name of me, there I am in the midst of them."

21. Then having come, the Petros said to him, "Lord, how often will the brother of me sin against me and I will forgive him? Up to seven times?"

22. The Yeshua says to him, "I do not say to you up to seven times, but up to seventy times seven.

23. Because of this the kingdom of the heavens has been likened to a man, a king, who wished to settle [an] account with the slaves of him.

24. And having begun to settle, one was brought to him owing ten thousand talents.

25. And not having [anything] to repay, the lord commanded him to be sold, and the wife and the children and all, as much as he had, and repayment to be made.

26. Having fallen therefore, the slave was worshiping him, saying, 'Have patience with me, and I will repay you all.'

27. And having been moved-with-compassion, the lord of the slave that released him and the loan forgave him.

28. But having gone out, the slave that found one of the fellow-slaves of him who owed him a hundred denarii, and having seized he was choking him, saying, 'Repay whatever you owe.'

29. Having fallen therefore, the fellow-slave of him was urging him, saying, 'Have patience with me, and I will repay you.'

30. But he did not wish; but having gone, he cast him into prison until he should repay what was owed.

31. Having seen therefore, the fellow-slaves of him what happened were greatly grieved, and having come they made clear to the lord of them all what happened.

32. Then having summoned him, the lord of him says to him, 'Evil slave, all that debt I forgave you, since you urged me.

33. Was it not necessary [for] you also to have mercy on the fellow-slave of you, as I also had mercy on you?'

34. And having been angered, the lord of him delivered him to the torturers until he should repay all that was owed to him.

35. So also the Father of me, the heavenly, will do to you, unless each of you forgives the brother of him from the hearts of you."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **σκάνδαλον (vv.6-9):** "Scandal/cause of stumbling" — rendered consistently as "scandalize" to preserve the Greek root. The word means a trap or snare that causes someone to fall — a cause of spiritual ruin.

2. **v.11 — Absent in Sinaiticus:** "For the Son of Man came to save the lost" does not appear in Sinaiticus. It appears to be a later addition harmonizing with Luke 19:10.

3. **μύλος ὀνικός (v.6):** "Millstone of a donkey" — a large millstone turned by a donkey, as opposed to the smaller hand-mill. The image is deliberately extreme: drowning with a massive stone around the neck.

4. **μυρίων ταλάντων (v.24):** "Ten thousand talents" — an absurdly enormous sum. One talent was roughly 6,000 denarii; 10,000 talents = 60 million denarii, or about 200,000 years of daily wages. The debt is unpayable by design — this is the point of the parable.

5. **ἑκατὸν δηνάρια (v.28):** "A hundred denarii" — about 100 days' wages. A real sum, but trivial compared to 10,000 talents. The contrast is devastating.

— EMET, AI Translator
